because the first thing's not really much value unless we sell it, unless it is abundantly clear, shoved in everybody's face, and rammed down their throat, which brings us to shameless self-promotion. That's what they're getting paid for. So going back to your Disney brand, the reason that that exists as a value is relentless, expansive, massive, ongoing promotion. That's why that exists as a value. Were it not for all the promotion, it wouldn't help us build value. So we get paid for that. We get paid for an extremely high tolerance for what most people would consider paying. A lot of people don't understand that. And in your questionnaires, sort of a reoccurring theme from everybody, but from a lot, was I would summarize it as saying, I'm afraid to get and sustain that level of income. I'm going to have to endure more pain. Now different people said it different ways, but ultimately that's what everybody was getting to. And you're right. Seven-figure people get paid for their tolerance for pain. Eight-figure people, that's about all they're getting paid for. Well, I mean, if you think about Trump, think about Trump. He's not getting paid anymore to make deals. Somebody else is making all the deals. He's not getting paid for that. So therefore, he's not getting paid for whatever skill or acumen or talent he has in real estate. That's not what he's getting paid for. He's certainly not getting paid to run a company. Somebody's managing all that, although he does sign the checks, which we'll talk about that. But what really is he getting paid for? He's getting paid for willing to be on close to 24-7, be in friendly environments and unfriendly environments, let people make fun of him, criticize him, attack him, as well as celebrate him, do things he doesn't particularly necessarily like doing inherently. He's willing to endure more pain, what some people would think is pain. Hearn had a really good comment in his questionnaire, one of the sections, how much time spent doing what you like. I've learned to like the things that are necessary. Well, there you are. I mean, but high-income earners don't get to do what they wanted to do, necessarily, originally. So Bill's brother-in-law, the dentist, which is brother-in-law, the dentist is here, so he's like the most famous dentist you've never met. We were talking before the seminar. So most dentists became dentists because of severe personality disorders. They don't like confrontation, they don't like persuasion, they don't want to sell, they still don't want to sell. They became dentists because they wanted to dentistry, that's why they went to dental school. Now, you understand dental school is a complete scam, fraud, lie, and illusion because the reason for going there, you're being trained to be a rotor-ruder guy. I mean, you're learning only the clinical, you're not learning anything else you need. So they come out of there and they want to do dentistry. But that's not, that is not how you get to high-six figures, slow-long seven figures. That's not where it's at. And so they either learn to like to do a bunch of other stuff they didn't think they were ever going to do, and for most of them that is painful. You know, we used to, I mentioned it before, we were doing practice management stuff with chiropractors in the late 70s and early 80s. And at the time, the average doctor was paying $35,000 to be in the program. And one of the biggest things we did with them was videotape them doing their presentation.
